# First-Pass Internal Memo

## Evidence Reviewed

Based on the provided records, treat the collection as a mixed corpus rather than one unified matter. The retrieved evidence includes a synthetic lien notice, a website design and hosting agreement, tobacco-litigation deposition materials, SEC filings, a broker-dealer audited report, and historical or public-record excerpts.

## Facts That Appear Supported

- The lien notice appears to identify HARBOR VIEW HOLDINGS, LLC as claimant and NORTHSTAR MARKET LLC as the party against whom the claim is asserted.
- The lien notice alleges unpaid access road maintenance charges totaling $2,850.00 for November and December 2023.
- The website agreement appears to identify Natalija Tunevic / FreeCook as client and Mitchell Vitalis / Mitchell's Web Advance, PLC as company.
- The deposition materials appear to include production obligations and subpoena timing rules, but several names and dates contain OCR uncertainty.
- The SEC and audited-report materials appear to be separate filings rather than documents from the same legal matter.

## Items Requiring Verification

- Verify the lien notice's certified-mail tracking digits, handwritten notes, blank signature/date/notary fields, and any filing deadline before relying on them.
- Check OCR-heavy subpoena and renotice pages against the original images before using party names, witness names, production topics, or service dates.
- Verify unusual financial values in the audited report, including the net-income figure that appears as `716.231`.
- Confirm whether any retrieved structured-field chunks are sufficient for the intended memo or whether page-level source quotes should be pulled before final review.

## Drafting Note

Use cautious, evidence-bound language for mixed-corpus runs. Prefer verification sections over definitive legal conclusions when the retrieved documents are unrelated or OCR quality is visibly degraded.

# Compliance Review Memo

## Executive Summary

The retrieved corpus combines six distinct document types: a real-property lien notice, a website services agreement, two tobacco-litigation deposition instruments, an SEC annual audited report (Form X-17A-5 Part III), and an SEC Form F-X appointment of agent. The materials do not arise from a single matter and should not be summarized as one transaction.

## Findings by Filing

### Lien Notice — HARBOR VIEW HOLDINGS, LLC v. NORTHSTAR MARKET LLC

Aggregate principal of $2,850.00 is asserted for unpaid access road maintenance covering November 2023 and December 2023. [e7a7d9482447de83:fields] Demand for payment was served by certified mail on January 9, 2024. [e7a7d9482447de83:fields] The fifteen-day cure period is recorded as expired; the lien itself is dated with a blank day in February 2024 and bears a handwritten "file by 03/15?" annotation that has not been confirmed as a recording deadline. [e7a7d9482447de83:fields]

### Website Design, Development and Hosting Agreement

The agreement, dated January 11, 2018, was entered between Natalija Tunevic (Client, FreeCook) and Mitchell Vitalis (Company, Mitchell's Web Advance, PLC). [4f7f73fad7d3f00c:fields] The contract price is $5,000, payable as a $1,900 prepayment and a $3,100 final installment on completion of the scope of work. [4f7f73fad7d3f00c:fields] Rush work is billed at the standard $40 hourly rate plus a 25% surcharge. [4f7f73fad7d3f00c:fields] The project window runs from February 8, 2018 to May 3, 2018. [4f7f73fad7d3f00c:fields]

### Tobacco-Litigation Discovery (Renotice + Subpoena)

The renotice obligates the deponent to produce, no later than five days before examination, the full set of books, records, computer data, correspondence, contracts, statements, handwritten notes, and other materials relating to the enumerated nicotine, pH, alkalinity, and tobacco-product topics. [d61bba9a2aabfec3:p4:c0] The deposition subpoena fixes production at the date and time shown in Item 1 but not earlier than twenty days after issuance or fifteen days after service, whichever is later. [fd38ffa95c4f3d59:p1:c1]

### SEC Annual Audited Report — Cole Capital Corporation

Total revenue is reported as 93,645,678 with total operating expenses of 92,994,616 and operating income of 651,062. [f30939d16de6e4bb:fields] Interest income is 65,169. [f30939d16de6e4bb:fields] The net income value renders as "716.231" in the structured field and must be reconciled to the source statements before being cited externally, since the punctuation pattern indicates either a transcription error or a thousands-separator artifact. [f30939d16de6e4bb:fields]

### SEC Form F-X — UFJ Shintaku Ginko Kabushiki Kaisha

The Form F-X identifies the registrant as UFJ Shintaku Ginko Kabushiki Kaisha (formerly Toyo Shintaku Ginko Kabushiki Kaisha) and Hiroyuki Fujiwara as agent for service of process. [f292902ed5f80a01:fields] The related Form CB was filed December 6, 2000; the F-X was signed in Tokyo on January 22, 2002. [f292902ed5f80a01:fields] Successor-agent obligations include six-year periods in the circumstances specified by the form. [f292902ed5f80a01:fields]

## Items Requiring Verification

- The "file by 03/15?" annotation on the lien notice has not been confirmed as a statutory or contractual deadline. [e7a7d9482447de83:fields]
- The Cole Capital net income value rendered as "716.231" is inconsistent with the magnitudes of the surrounding revenue and expense lines and must be reconciled before being used in any external communication. [f30939d16de6e4bb:fields]
- All OCR-rendered subpoena and renotice pages contain replacement characters; verify party names, witness names, production topics, and service dates against original images before quoting in correspondence.

## Operator Directive

Maintain full-prose, page-and-line citation precision. Every factual claim must carry an inline citation. Reserve cautionary language ("appears", "provisional") for items called out in the verification list above.

# Case Fact Summary

Draft type: case_fact_summary

## Review Flags

- Several claims rely on derived-fields evidence; operator should verify those facts against the full official text.
- The source excerpts include omission/redaction markers such as “[…]”, so no facts were inferred from omitted text.

## Document Identification

- The supplied document is the Final Report of the Hearing Officer in Case AT.40437, Apple App Store Practices (music streaming), published as Official Journal C/2024/3556 with EEA relevance. [b56a4cec78b14580:p1:c0]

## Parties And Subject

- The draft decision concerns Apple Inc. and Apple Distribution International Limited, collectively referred to as Apple, and certain App Store provisions applicable to music streaming service app developers. [b56a4cec78b14580:p1:c0]

## Commission Theory

- The Commission described the provisions at issue as Anti-Steering Provisions that prevent music streaming service providers from informing iOS users about alternative subscription options outside the providers’ iOS apps and from allowing effective choice between alternative subscription possibilities. [b56a4cec78b14580:p1:c0]
- The document states that the Commission concluded Apple’s conduct amounted to an imposition of unfair trading conditions within the meaning of Article 102(a) TFEU, detrimental to iOS users’ interests. [b56a4cec78b14580:p1:c0]

## Procedural History

- Spotify contacted the Commission starting in 2013 regarding Apple conduct affecting music streaming service providers active in the App Store; meetings and calls with Spotify and Apple followed in 2015 and later years. [b56a4cec78b14580:p1:c1]
- Spotify submitted a formal complaint on 11 March 2019 under Article 7 of Regulation No. 1/2003, followed by an amended final version on 9 April 2019. [b56a4cec78b14580:p1:c2]
- The first Statement of Objections was issued on 30 April 2021 and was replaced by a supplementary Statement of Objections on 28 February 2023. [b56a4cec78b14580:p1:c1]
- The extracted fields state that the Commission initiated proceedings on 16 June 2020; Apple replied to the first Statement of Objections on 17 September 2021; Apple replied to the Replacement Statement of Objections on 19 May 2023; an oral hearing occurred on 30 June 2023; and the Hearing Officer report was signed in Brussels on 1 March 2024. [b56a4cec78b14580:fields]

## Spotify Allegations

- Spotify alleged that Apple prevented Spotify from advertising its Premium option within, and to some extent outside, the app, thereby restricting promotion of cheaper subscription possibilities outside the iOS environment to iOS users. [b56a4cec78b14580:p1:c2]

## Access To File And Deadlines

- The extracted fields state that Apple initially had 12 weeks to reply to the first Statement of Objections, received an extension to 3 September 2021, and then received a further extension to 17 September 2021. [b56a4cec78b14580:fields]
- The extracted fields state that Apple initially had 8 weeks to reply to the Replacement Statement of Objections from 7 March 2023, requested an extension until 1 June 2023, and received an extension only until 19 May 2023. [b56a4cec78b14580:fields]

## Letter Of Facts

- The extracted fields state that the Commission issued a Letter of Facts on 6 December 2023, Apple received access to additional file material on 7 December and 15 December 2023, and Apple replied to the Letter of Facts on 12 January 2024. [b56a4cec78b14580:fields]
- The extracted fields state that the Letter of Facts said the Commission was considering adjusting fine calculation using value of sales based only on App Store commission fees paid by music streaming providers to Apple in the EEA, and possibly applying point 37 of the Fining Guidelines to impose an additional lump sum. [b56a4cec78b14580:fields]
- No specific fine amount is stated in the supplied text according to the extracted fields. [b56a4cec78b14580:fields]

## Apple Procedural Arguments

- In its reply to the Replacement Statement of Objections, Apple argued that the RSO failed to meet the burden of proof because it relied on outdated and incomplete information and data and because the Commission had not undertaken new investigative measures before adopting the RSO. [b56a4cec78b14580:p9:c2]
- Apple argued in reply to the Letter of Facts that, aside from two RFIs to Spotify for data updates, the Commission took no formal new investigative steps since March 2020. [b56a4cec78b14580:p9:c2]
- Apple argued that the Commission unduly influenced a Spotify consumer survey by suggesting it, preparing the first draft, emphasizing that it should demonstrate lock-in effects, and directing it through multiple calls and meetings. [b56a4cec78b14580:p10:c2]
- Apple argued that even if the Case Team drafted minutes contemporaneously, it was implausible that Spotify’s comments could accurately reflect the discussions that took place. [b56a4cec78b14580:p13:c2]

## Hearing Officer Observations

- The Hearing Officer observed that the sufficiency of proof of an Article 102 TFEU infringement, including the probative value of specific evidence, is a matter for the substantive assessment of the case. [b56a4cec78b14580:p10:c2]
- The Hearing Officer observed that the Commission issued a Letter of Facts providing updated evidence for most factual findings Apple considered outdated. [b56a4cec78b14580:p10:c3]

## Open Questions

- Verify all procedural dates and deadline extensions against the full source PDF or official record because several timeline facts are taken from extracted fields rather than source-page text.
- Verify whether the Official Journal publication date is 7 June 2024 and whether the full citation should be OJ C/2024/3556, because the supplied source-page excerpts show the OJ reference but not all publication metadata in one place.
